refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. The p95 metric ensures consistent performance under realistic network variability. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning network, vehicle, and control systems.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (command transmission + vehicle response + telemetry return) under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with safety manager oversight. Log p95 latency across representative operational scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (position, status, sensor data) back to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the safety system.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a dedicated low-latency network protocol (e.g., UDP-based custom protocol) with priority queuing and adaptive compression to minimize command and telemetry transmission time.
DP-B: Deploy a hybrid architecture combining standard IP networking with edge computing (local gateway) to reduce round-trip distance and enable predictive command buffering.
DP-C: Use a commercial teleoperation middleware (e.g., ROS 2 with DDS) with QoS tuning and network optimization to achieve latency targets.
DP-D: Implement a multi-path redundant network with automatic failover and latency monitoring to ensure p95 compliance across network variability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Deploy a hybrid architecture combining standard IP networking with edge computing (local gateway) to reduce round-trip distance and enable predictive command buffering, coupled with DP-A's low-latency protocol for command/telemetry exchange and DP-D's latency monitoring for safety compliance.

potential_sources: Network infrastructure design (external dependency on site network conditions)
Vehicle control system responsiveness (coupled to FR-NGHE-008.2)
Sensor and actuator hardware latency (physical constraints)
Safety system integration (must receive latency violation alerts)
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP is primarily decoupled from vehicle mechanical design but tightly coupled to network architecture and control system timing. Safety criticality requires explicit latency monitoring and failsafe mechanisms. Hybrid approach mitigates single-point-of-failure risk in network path.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Site network infrastructure supports UDP and custom protocol deployment without interference.
Assumption: Edge gateway hardware can be deployed and maintained at the worksite.
Risk: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters, causing p95 violations despite optimal design.
Risk: Predictive command buffering may introduce safety issues if predictions diverge from operator intent.
Risk: Multi-path redundancy adds complexity and potential for race conditions in command ordering.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP to explicitly include: (1) latency budget allocation across network, vehicle, and sensor subsystems; (2) fallback mode definition (e.g., autonomous hold or manual override) if latency exceeds threshold; (3) network condition monitoring and operator notification protocol.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance, including command transmission protocol, edge gateway logic, telemetry aggregation, and latency monitoring. Interfaces with Vehicle for command execution and telemetry collection, with Safety Services for latency violation alerts, and with Network Link for transport.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator console; must be transmitted to Vehicle with ≤75 ms latency.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, status) back to tele-operator; must be delivered with ≤75 ms latency.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports latency violations, network degradation, or failsafe triggers to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency response.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (IP, cellular, or hybrid); Teleoperation module optimizes protocol and routing to meet latency targets within available bandwidth and reliability.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end latency performance. Command transmission, edge gateway, telemetry aggregation, and latency monitoring are core responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only latency violation alerts diffuse to Safety Services.

Vehicle owns command execution latency (FR-NGHE-008.2) but not end-to-end latency. Assigning full FR to Vehicle would scatter network and gateway logic across modules, violating cohesion. Vehicle should own only its internal control loop timing.
Safety Services consumes latency violation alerts but does not own latency performance. Assigning FR to Safety Services would create circular dependency and diffuse latency optimization logic.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but too coarse-grained for implementation. Assigning FR here defers architectural decisions and prevents clear ownership and accountability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module contains all logic and interfaces necessary to achieve end-to-end latency targets: command reception, protocol optimization, edge gateway coordination, telemetry aggregation, and latency monitoring. Vehicle module is a consumer of Tasking and producer of Telemetry; Safety Services is a consumer of SafetyAlert. Network Link is the underlying transport layer.
containment_risks: Risk: Edge gateway hardware deployment and maintenance may fall outside Teleoperation module scope if treated as infrastructure; recommend explicit ownership assignment.
Risk: Vehicle control loop timing (FR-NGHE-008.2) must be co-designed with Teleoperation to ensure end-to-end latency; recommend interface SLA definition.
Risk: Network Link variability is external; Teleoperation must implement adaptive mechanisms (compression, buffering, failover) to maintain p95 under agreed conditions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify ownership boundaries: (1) Teleoperation owns protocol, gateway logic, and monitoring; (2) Vehicle owns command execution timing and commits to ≤50 ms p95; (3) Safety Services owns failsafe response to latency violations; (4) Infrastructure team owns edge gateway hardware deployment and network provisioning.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (hybrid architecture with low-latency protocol and latency monitoring) directly addresses FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms. DP is specific, measurable, and feasible. Coupling check identifies external dependencies (network, vehicle control) but no fundamental contradictions. DP includes fallback and monitoring mechanisms to handle safety criticality.

diffusion_risks: Latency monitoring logic may diffuse to Safety Services if not clearly scoped; recommend explicit interface contract (SafetyAlert).
Edge gateway coordination may diffuse to Vehicle or Infrastructure if ownership is ambiguous; recommend Teleoperation owns gateway logic, Vehicle owns local command execution.
Network optimization (compression, buffering) must remain in Teleoperation; risk of diffusion to Vehicle if not carefully architected.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score. All critical latency-related logic (protocol, gateway, monitoring) is cohesive within this boundary. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. Ownership is clear and accountable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: measure end-to-end latency under agreed network conditions using instrumented test harness. Collect p95 statistics across representative scenarios. Conduct operator acceptance testing with safety manager oversight. Automated monitoring in production provides continuous verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR allocation to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) map to specific DP elements: command transmission (DP-A), vehicle execution (DP-B), telemetry return (DP-A), and monitoring (DP-D). Each sub-FR has a clear owner and verification method.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP to include explicit latency budget allocation: command transmission ≤75 ms, vehicle execution ≤50 ms, telemetry return ≤75 ms. Document assumptions about network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Define interface SLA between Teleoperation and Vehicle for command execution latency. Include acceptance criteria and test procedures.
Specify failsafe behavior if latency exceeds threshold: operator notification, autonomous hold, manual override, or graceful degradation. Coordinate with Safety Services.
Document edge gateway deployment model: location, hardware specs, maintenance responsibility, and network integration.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and has been successfully decomposed into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) that address distinct aspects of end-to-end latency: command transmission, vehicle execution, telemetry return, and monitoring. Each sub-FR is more specific and testable than the parent. DP and IM assignments are sound and pass gate review. Proceeding to next decomposition level will clarify implementation details, interface contracts, and ownership boundaries. Safety criticality and latency constraints justify detailed decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (position, status, sensor data) back to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the safety system.

assumptions: Site network infrastructure supports UDP and custom protocol deployment.
Edge gateway hardware can be deployed and maintained at the worksite.
Vehicle control system can achieve ≤50 ms command execution latency.
Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Tele-operator has sufficient situational awareness with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms.
Predictive command buffering does not introduce safety risks if predictions diverge from operator intent.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters, causing p95 violations.
Edge gateway deployment complexity and maintenance burden may exceed initial estimates.
Multi-path redundancy may introduce race conditions in command ordering or state consistency.
Operator acceptance of latency-induced control lag under stress conditions is unvalidated.
Integration with Safety Services failsafe mechanisms requires detailed design and testing.
evidence_needed: Network condition baseline measurements at representative worksites.
Vehicle control system latency characterization (command reception to actuator response).
Operator acceptance testing results with safety manager sign-off.
Edge gateway hardware and deployment cost/schedule estimates.
Failsafe mode definition and safety case documentation.
Latency monitoring and alerting system design and test results.
